content: Disease surveillance systems systematically collect, analyze, and disseminate health data for public health action. Passive surveillance relies on routine reporting from healthcare providers (e.g., notifiable disease reports)—cost-effective but suffers from underreporting. Active surveillance involves proactive case-finding through regular outreach to providers or laboratories—more complete but resource-intensive. Sentinel surveillance monitors specific sites or populations to detect trends (e.g., influenza-like illness networks). Syndromic surveillance uses pre-diagnostic data (ER chief complaints, OTC medication sales) for early outbreak detection.
source: CDC surveillance guidelines, WHO Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network
